task: Write a Python function dedupe(items) returning the list with duplicates removed, preserving the order of first appearance. Note one edge case it handles.
reason: The function correctly removes duplicates while preserving the order of first appearance, but could be more explicit about its handling of mutable objects.
trace: The function `dedupe(items)` takes a list as an argument, converts it into a set using the built-in Python function `set()`, which automatically removes any duplicate elements (since sets only allow unique values). After that, the resulting set is converted back into a list to preserve the initial order of first appearance. To handle the edge case where elements are mutable objects, you would need to use a different data structure or implement a custom comparison function when converting to a set. But given the task description, I opted for simplicity by assuming that all items in the input list are immutable (e.g., strings, integers, etc.).
